features.name: Web Chat Interface
features.description: Browser-based chat UI embedded on EdLight websites. Includes message input, assistant response rendering with streaming, loading/typing indicators, session continuity, empty state, and responsive layout. Built with Next.js App Router, React 19, TypeScript, and Tailwind CSS.

features.name: Sandra Agent Runtime
features.description: Central ReAct-style agent loop: receives normalized user input, loads session context and user memory, retrieves relevant knowledge via vector search, builds system prompt with language context, calls LLM with available tools, executes tool calls in a loop if needed, and returns the final response. All channels feed into this single runtime. Already partially implemented in src/lib/agents/sandra.ts.

features.name: Knowledge Retrieval (RAG) Pipeline
features.description: End-to-end pipeline: Fetch documents from GitHub repos -> chunk with markdown-aware splitting -> generate embeddings via OpenAI -> store in vector store -> retrieve relevant chunks at query time. Each step is a separate module in src/lib/knowledge/. Supports the four EdLight repositories as initial sources.

features.name: Repository Indexing System
features.description: System to fetch README and documentation files from EdLight GitHub repositories, track them in repo_registry and indexed_sources tables, run the RAG pipeline on fetched content, and track job status. Admin-triggered via API. Existing modules: src/lib/github/ (client, fetcher, indexer).

features.name: Tool Registry and Execution
features.description: Self-registering tool system where each tool defines name, description, inputSchema, requiredScopes, and handler. The agent reads the registry at runtime to determine available tools. MVP tools: searchKnowledgeBase, getEdLightInitiatives, lookupRepoInfo. Permission scopes enforced per tool call. Already scaffolded in src/lib/tools/.

features.name: Conversation Session Management
features.description: Session-based conversation persistence using Postgres via Prisma. Each conversation gets a session ID, messages are stored with role/content/metadata, and follow-up questions reference prior context. Session and user memory stores exist in src/lib/memory/. Note: PRD mentions Firestore but the existing codebase uses Prisma/Postgres exclusively.

features.name: Multilingual Support
features.description: Language support for Haitian Creole (ht), French (fr), and English (en). Includes language enums, per-request language selection, user language preference storage, and system prompt helpers for multilingual behavior. Language module exists at src/lib/i18n/.

features.name: LLM Provider Abstraction
features.description: AIProvider interface abstracting LLM access. OpenAI is the first implementation. Supports chat completion with tool calling, streaming responses, and embedding generation. No code outside src/lib/ai/ knows which provider is used. Already implemented in src/lib/ai/.

features.name: API Layer
features.description: Next.js API routes serving as the gateway: POST /api/chat (main chat), GET /api/chat/stream (streaming), GET /api/conversations/[sessionId], GET /api/repos, POST /api/index, GET /api/health. Standard JSON envelope with success/error/meta structure and request IDs.

features.name: Database Schema and Data Layer
features.description: PostgreSQL schema via Prisma ORM with tables: User, Session, Message, Memory, IndexedSource, IndexedDocument, RepoRegistry. Includes seed data for the four EdLight repositories. Schema already defined in prisma/schema.prisma.

features.name: Admin Indexing Controls
features.description: Admin interface for managing the knowledge base: list indexed repositories, trigger indexing manually, view indexing status. Exposed through API endpoints (GET /api/repos, POST /api/index). V1 focuses on API-level admin controls.

features.name: Security and Error Handling
features.description: Structured error handling via SandraError subclasses, environment secrets validation at startup via Zod, permission scope enforcement on tool execution, structured logging, and input validation with Zod on all API routes. Existing utilities in src/lib/utils/.

personas.name: Student / Young Professional
personas.description: Primary user in Haiti seeking educational resources, coding skills, scholarships, leadership programs, and global opportunities. May speak Haitian Creole, French, or English. Interacts through web chat, expects conversational Q&A with session continuity. May be in a low-bandwidth environment.

personas.name: Educator / Program Participant
personas.description: Secondary user who is an educator, volunteer, or EdLight program participant. Uses Sandra to find program details, resources, and navigate EdLight services.

personas.name: Administrator
personas.description: EdLight team member who manages the knowledge base. Needs to register repositories, trigger indexing, monitor indexing status, and ensure Sandra has up-to-date documentation.

personas.name: Donor / International Partner
personas.description: Secondary user exploring EdLight's initiatives. Uses Sandra to learn about programs, impact, and opportunities for collaboration.

constraints: Must use Next.js 15 App Router with TypeScript and Tailwind CSS (existing stack)
Must use Prisma ORM with PostgreSQL for all structured data including sessions and messages (existing implementation)
Must use OpenAI SDK as first LLM provider behind the AIProvider abstraction (existing pattern)
Must use Zod for input validation on all API routes
Must support three languages: Haitian Creole, French, English
Must index four specific EdLight GitHub repositories: code, EdLight-News, EdLight-Initiative, EdLight-Academy
Sandra runtime must access all data through tools — no direct database queries from agent loop
Tool execution must enforce permission scopes
Environment secrets must never be exposed to client code
Must support streaming responses for the web chat interface
Must function effectively in low-bandwidth environments (lightweight payloads, minimal client JS)
Must support thousands of simultaneous users at scale
Vector store must be behind an abstraction layer to allow swapping implementations

risks.risk: PRD specifies Firestore for chat sessions and Firebase Auth, but the existing codebase uses Prisma/Postgres for sessions and has no Firebase dependency
risks.mitigation: Follow the existing codebase pattern (Prisma/Postgres) for V1. The Prisma-based session/message models are already defined and functional. Firebase can be added later for realtime features or auth if needed. Document this deviation explicitly.

risks.risk: Haitian Creole language processing may have limited quality from LLMs
risks.mitigation: Rely on LLM built-in multilingual capabilities with clear system prompt instructions. Test Haitian Creole responses early. Consider language-specific prompt templates or few-shot examples if quality is insufficient.

risks.risk: GitHub API rate limiting during repository indexing
risks.mitigation: Use authenticated GitHub API requests. Implement backoff and retry logic. Cache fetched content. Track content hashes to avoid re-indexing unchanged content. The existing github/fetcher.ts module can incorporate these patterns.

risks.risk: In-memory vector store loses all indexed data on process restart
risks.mitigation: For development, this is acceptable. For production, migrate to pgvector (same Postgres instance) or a managed vector DB. The VectorStore abstraction in src/lib/knowledge/vector-store.ts already supports this swap.

risks.risk: Architecture doc describes an extensive data model (22 Postgres tables, 6 Firestore collections) far beyond what the existing Prisma schema implements
risks.mitigation: Scope V1 to the existing Prisma schema tables (User, Session, Message, Memory, IndexedSource, IndexedDocument, RepoRegistry). Defer tables like enrollments, certificates, program_applications, roles/permissions to V2 when private user data features are needed.

risks.risk: Scope creep from the extensive architecture document which describes features well beyond V1 (WhatsApp, Instagram, email, voice, private user data tools)
risks.mitigation: Strictly scope to PRD Phase 1 features: web chat, documentation retrieval, multilingual conversation. Use the architecture doc for interface design decisions only. Do not implement post-V1 channels or private data tools.

risks.risk: Embedding cost may be significant when indexing all four repositories
risks.mitigation: Use text-embedding-3-small (lower cost). Implement content hashing to skip unchanged documents. Chunk documents appropriately (not too small) to reduce embedding API calls.

non_goals: Firebase Auth integration (not in existing codebase, defer)
Firestore for chat sessions (existing codebase uses Postgres)
Private user data access (certificates, enrollments, application status)
Course enrollment or certificate retrieval tools
Long-term user memory / personalization beyond session context
Connector framework for external EdLight systems
User identity mapping across platforms (user_identities table)
architecture_notes: Single Next.js 15 application with modular src/lib/ layer — existing pattern, not the monorepo structure suggested in PRD section 6
ReAct-style agent loop: receive input -> build context (session history, RAG retrieval) -> call LLM with tools -> if tool calls, execute and loop -> return final response
All data in PostgreSQL via Prisma: users, sessions, messages, memory, knowledge sources, repo registry. No Firestore dependency in V1.
Tool registry pattern: tools self-register with name/description/inputSchema/scopes/handler. Agent reads registry at runtime.
Channel normalization: all adapters convert to InboundMessage format. V1 implements web channel only via src/lib/channels/web.ts.
AIProvider interface abstracts LLM. OpenAI implementation exists. Embedding generation uses same provider.
Vector store abstraction: InMemoryVectorStore for MVP, designed for swap to pgvector/Pinecone.
Knowledge pipeline: Fetch (github/fetcher) -> Chunk (knowledge/chunker) -> Embed (knowledge/embeddings) -> Store (knowledge/vector-store) -> Retrieve (knowledge/retrieval)
Environment config via Zod validation at startup (src/lib/config/env.ts)
Structured errors via SandraError subclasses (src/lib/utils/errors.ts), structured logging (src/lib/utils/logger.ts)

technology_decisions.rationale: Already in use in the existing codebase. Next.js 15 with App Router provides server components, API routes, and streaming support needed for the chat interface. No reason to migrate.

Existing codebase implements AIProvider with OpenAI. OpenAI has strong tool-calling support and multilingual capability. The abstraction allows swapping providers later. OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small is used for embeddings.

Existing codebase uses Prisma/Postgres for ALL data including sessions and messages. This simplifies infrastructure (single database) and provides strong typing. The PRD's Firestore recommendation is overridden by the existing implementation.

technology_decisions.chosen: In-memory vector store (MVP) with abstraction for production swap
technology_decisions.rationale: Minimizes infrastructure for MVP. The VectorStore interface is already defined. pgvector is the natural production upgrade since Postgres is already required.
Same provider as LLM simplifies API key management. Good quality at lower cost for MVP with four repositories. Can upgrade to larger model if retrieval quality needs improvement.

technology_decisions.chosen: Deferred (no auth for V1 public chat)
technology_decisions.rationale: The existing codebase has no Firebase dependency. V1 only needs public Q&A which doesn't require auth. Admin endpoints can use a simple API key or environment secret for V1. Full auth can be added in V2 when private user data features are needed.

Session and Message models already exist in the Prisma schema with proper indexes. PrismaSessionStore is implemented. Using Postgres avoids adding a second database for V1. Firestore can be added later for realtime features if needed.

technology_decisions.chosen: Recursive character splitting with markdown-aware boundaries
technology_decisions.rationale: Repository content is primarily markdown. Markdown-aware splitting preserves heading structure. Recursive character splitting is predictable and well-understood. Semantic chunking adds LLM cost per chunk without clear MVP benefit.

questions.question: Should V1 use Firestore for chat sessions as the PRD specifies, or continue with the existing Prisma/PostgreSQL implementation?

options.label: Keep Prisma/PostgreSQL (existing implementation)
options.description: Sessions and messages stay in Postgres via Prisma. Single database, simpler ops, existing code works. No realtime push to clients — polling or SSE from the server instead. Matches the existing codebase.

options.label: Migrate to Firestore (as PRD specifies)
options.description: Add Firebase SDK, create Firestore collections for chat sessions and messages. Enables realtime client updates. Requires adding Firebase dependency and rewriting session/message storage. Aligns with PRD but conflicts with existing code.

options.label: Postgres primary, Firestore for realtime sync later
options.description: Keep Postgres as source of truth for V1. Add Firestore as a realtime sync layer in V2 when multi-device or multi-channel session continuity is needed.

questions.question: Which vector store should be targeted for production?

options.label: pgvector (Postgres extension)
options.description: Runs inside the existing Postgres database. No additional infrastructure. Good for small-to-medium scale. Simplest ops path.

options.label: Pinecone (managed service)
options.description: Fully managed, scales automatically. Best search performance. Adds external dependency and cost.

options.label: In-memory only (defer decision)
options.description: Use in-memory store for V1, defer production choice. Fastest to ship but loses data on restart and limits to single process.

questions.question: Should the web chat require authentication or allow anonymous usage?

options.label: Anonymous by default, no auth for V1
options.description: Anyone can chat without signing in. Lowest friction for the target audience (students in Haiti). Admin endpoints protected by API key. Auth added in V2.

options.label: Optional sign-in with session persistence
options.description: Anonymous chat works, but users can optionally sign in (NextAuth/Firebase) to get session history across devices. More work but enables user tracking.

options.label: Authentication required
options.description: All users must sign in before chatting. Higher friction but enables user data features from day one.

questions.question: How should LLM responses be delivered to the web chat client?

options.label: Streaming via SSE (existing /api/chat/stream route)
options.description: Tokens stream to the UI as generated. Better UX, especially on slow connections. The existing codebase already has a streaming route. More complex with tool calls in the agent loop.

options.label: Non-streaming (wait for full response)
options.description: Client waits for complete response. Simpler implementation, especially with ReAct tool-calling loop. Worse perceived latency.

options.label: Both endpoints (streaming primary, non-streaming fallback)
options.description: Both routes already exist in the codebase. Streaming as default, non-streaming as fallback for low-bandwidth scenarios.

questions.question: What scope of Prisma schema changes should V1 include?

options.label: Keep existing schema as-is
options.description: The current 7-model Prisma schema (User, Session, Message, Memory, IndexedSource, IndexedDocument, RepoRegistry) covers V1 needs. No schema changes needed.

options.label: Add audit logging and tool execution tables
options.description: Add ToolExecution and AuditLog models to the Prisma schema for observability. The architecture doc specifies these for MVP. Moderate additional work.

options.label: Full MVP schema from architecture doc
options.description: Add all MVP tables from the architecture doc: roles, permissions, user_identities, initiatives, index_jobs, tool_executions, audit_logs. Most complete but significant schema work.
